48.intro:
Introduction

The partograph is the most important monitoring tool in the labour room. In India, where a significant proportion of deliveries occur in primary health centres and community settings, the partograph enables the nurse midwife to detect slow progress early and refer in time. Its use is mandatory under the National Health Mission (NHM) protocol for all institutional deliveries.
48.conclusion:
Conclusion

The partograph is a powerful, low-cost tool that has proven effective in reducing maternal and perinatal mortality by detecting prolonged and obstructed labour before complications arise. The nurse midwife must be skilled in plotting and interpreting every component — fetal, progress, and maternal — to guide timely referral and intervention as required by MUHS and INC competency standards.

50.def:
Definition

Active Management of Third Stage of Labour (AMTSL): A WHO-recommended package of three interventions administered immediately after birth to accelerate placental delivery and prevent postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) — the leading cause of maternal death in India — comprising a uterotonic (oxytocin 10 IU IM within 1 minute), controlled cord traction, and uterine massage.
50.conclusion:
Conclusion

AMTSL is the single most effective intervention to prevent PPH and reduce maternal deaths. The nurse midwife must perform all three steps — oxytocin, controlled cord traction with counter-traction, and uterine massage — correctly and in sequence for every delivery. This practice is central to MUHS competency, NHM safe-delivery guidelines, and the Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY) protocol.

52.intro:
Introduction

For the fetus to be born vaginally, its head must negotiate the curved birth canal by performing a series of passive positional adjustments collectively called the mechanism of labour or cardinal movements. Understanding these movements is fundamental for the nurse midwife to anticipate normal progress, detect deviation, and conduct delivery safely. The most common and favourable position is Left Occiput Anterior (LOA).
52.conclusion:
Conclusion

The mechanism of labour demonstrates the remarkable adaptation of the fetal head to the pelvic architecture through eight sequential movements. A nurse midwife who understands each cardinal movement can conduct a safe delivery, recognise deviations such as deep transverse arrest, and intervene or refer in a timely manner — key competencies emphasised by MUHS and the Indian Nursing Council (INC).

53.def:
Definition

Second stage of labour: The period from complete cervical dilatation (10 cm) to the delivery of the baby, lasting approximately 1–2 hours in a primigravida and about 30 minutes in a multigravida, during which the mother actively bears down with contractions to expel the fetus.
53.conclusion:
Conclusion

Skilled care in the second stage protects both mother and baby. The nurse midwife must confirm full dilatation before permitting active pushing, monitor the fetal heart every 5 minutes, support the perineum to prevent tears, and conduct a controlled, step-by-step delivery — followed immediately by skin-to-skin care and early breastfeeding. These MUHS and INC competencies are pivotal to safe motherhood outcomes.

54.intro:
Introduction

The first stage of labour is the longest and most demanding period for the labouring woman, stretching from the onset of true labour to full cervical dilatation at 10 cm. In India, many maternal deaths and near-misses occur because abnormalities in the first stage go unrecognised. Systematic, partograph-guided management by the nurse midwife is therefore the cornerstone of safe labour care.
54.conclusion:
Conclusion

Effective management of the first stage combines continuous monitoring with the partograph, comfort care, bladder attention, and vigilance for danger signs. The nurse midwife must start the partograph at 4 cm, monitor FHR every 15–30 minutes, and act promptly when the dilatation line crosses the alert or action line — skills central to MUHS examination competencies and the NHM safe-delivery protocol.

55.def:
Definition

Third stage of labour: The period from the birth of the baby to the complete expulsion of the placenta and membranes, normally lasting 5–15 minutes (maximum 30 minutes), during which placental separation occurs and is confirmed by a gush of blood, cord lengthening, and a globular uterus.
55.conclusion:
Conclusion

The third stage, though brief, is the most dangerous for the mother because of the risk of postpartum haemorrhage. Active management with oxytocin, controlled cord traction, and uterine massage — the AMTSL protocol — is the gold standard endorsed by WHO and India’s NHM. The nurse midwife’s ability to recognise signs of placental separation and complete the third stage safely is a core MUHS competency.

56.def:
Definition

Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH): Excessive blood loss of 500 ml or more after a vaginal delivery (or 1000 ml after caesarean section) within 24 hours of birth, most commonly caused by uterine atony; prevented primarily by uterotonics such as oxytocin, ergometrine, misoprostol, and carboprost.
56.conclusion:
Conclusion

PPH remains the leading cause of maternal mortality in India. The nurse midwife must know the correct drug, dose, route, and timing for each uterotonic agent — particularly oxytocin 10 IU IM as part of AMTSL — and be prepared to escalate to misoprostol or ergometrine when first-line therapy is insufficient. Early recognition and prompt uterotonic use are the MUHS and NHM keywords that save lives.

57.intro:
Introduction

Retained placenta is one of the most serious complications of the third stage and a leading contributor to postpartum haemorrhage and maternal mortality in India. The nurse midwife must recognise a placenta that has not delivered within 30 minutes, differentiate the type of retention, and initiate timely management while preparing for manual removal if needed.
57.conclusion:
Conclusion

Retained placenta demands swift recognition and a systematic approach: check for bladder distension, attempt controlled cord traction, and if the placenta is not delivered within 30 minutes, arrange for manual removal of placenta (MRP) under anaesthesia by a doctor. The nurse midwife’s role in early identification, IV access, oxytocin administration, and timely referral is a critical MUHS competency that directly prevents maternal death from haemorrhage.

58.intro:
Introduction

Episiotomy and perineal tears are among the most common injuries in obstetric practice. Proper, timely repair of the perineum after delivery is essential to restore anatomy, prevent haemorrhage, minimise infection, and ensure the woman’s comfort and long-term pelvic floor function. The nurse midwife in India is trained to perform and repair episiotomies as a core skilled-birth-attendant competency under MUHS and the NHM.
58.conclusion:
Conclusion

A correctly performed three-layer perineal repair, followed by diligent post-operative nursing care — hygiene, pain relief, sitz bath, REEDA assessment, and health education — ensures rapid, complication-free healing. The nurse midwife must be competent in recognising and grading perineal tears, repairing episiotomies, and detecting signs of infection or wound breakdown, as expected by MUHS and INC standards.

59.intro:
Introduction

Shoulder dystocia is a sudden, unpredictable obstetric emergency where the anterior shoulder of the fetus becomes impacted behind the maternal pubic symphysis after delivery of the head, preventing further descent. It is associated with macrosomia and gestational diabetes and demands immediate, coordinated action. Every nurse midwife must be drilled in its recognition and the McRoberts manoeuvre-based algorithm, as delay of even a few minutes risks brachial plexus injury and neonatal asphyxia.
59.def:
Definition

Shoulder dystocia: An obstetric emergency in which, after delivery of the fetal head, the anterior shoulder fails to deliver spontaneously and requires additional obstetric manoeuvres; it occurs when the biacromial diameter of the shoulders is impacted against the maternal pubic symphysis or sacral promontory.
59.conclusion:
Conclusion

Shoulder dystocia is a true obstetric emergency that requires immediate, calm, and stepwise management — call for help, McRoberts, suprapubic pressure, and internal manoeuvres — all performed within minutes to prevent fetal brain damage or death. The nurse midwife must recognise the “turtle sign”, initiate the HELPERR algorithm, and document every intervention accurately — essential MUHS emergency-obstetrics competencies.

60.intro:
Introduction

Uterine rupture is one of the most catastrophic obstetric emergencies, carrying a high risk of maternal and perinatal death. In India it is most commonly seen in women with a previous caesarean section scar, obstructed labour, or injudicious use of oxytocin. The nurse midwife must be able to recognise the warning signs early — particularly an impending rupture — and act without delay because every minute counts.
60.def:
Definition

Uterine rupture: A full-thickness tear through the uterine wall (myometrium and serosa) occurring most commonly during labour, classified as complete (involving peritoneum, with extrusion of fetus or placenta) or incomplete (peritoneum intact); it is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate surgical intervention.
60.conclusion:
Conclusion

Uterine rupture is largely preventable through careful screening for previous uterine surgery, judicious use of oxytocin, and vigilant partograph monitoring to detect obstructed labour before it becomes catastrophic. The nurse midwife’s responsibility is to recognise the warning signs — a pathological retraction ring, sudden cessation of contractions, and maternal collapse — call the obstetrician immediately, establish IV access, and prepare for emergency laparotomy. These actions are core MUHS emergency-obstetrics competencies.

98.intro:
Introduction

Induction of labour is one of the most frequently performed obstetric interventions, used when the risk of continuing the pregnancy outweighs the risk of delivery. In Indian hospitals under the NHM, post-term pregnancy and pre-eclampsia are the two most common indications. The nurse midwife plays a key role in cervical assessment using the Bishop score, drug administration, and continuous fetal monitoring during the induction process.
98.def:
Definition

Induction of labour: The artificial initiation of uterine contractions before their spontaneous onset, after the period of viability, with the aim of achieving vaginal delivery when continuing the pregnancy poses a greater risk to the mother or fetus than the process of delivery itself.
98.conclusion:
Conclusion

Induction of labour, when properly indicated and conducted, is a safe and life-saving intervention. The nurse midwife must assess cervical favourability using the Bishop score, administer uterotonics or assist with amniotomy as prescribed, and monitor FHR and contractions meticulously using the partograph to detect hyperstimulation or fetal distress without delay. These competencies are central to MUHS and INC expectations for skilled maternity care.

99.intro:
Introduction

Caesarean section (CS) is the surgical delivery of the fetus through incisions in the abdominal wall and uterus. In India the CS rate has risen sharply, making it imperative that the nurse midwife understands its indications, surgical considerations, and the comprehensive pre- and post-operative nursing care required for maternal and neonatal safety. It accounts for approximately 21% of institutional deliveries nationally and a higher proportion in urban tertiary centres.
99.def:
Definition

Caesarean section: A surgical procedure in which the fetus, placenta, and membranes are delivered through incisions made in the abdominal wall (laparotomy) and the uterus (hysterotomy), performed under regional or general anaesthesia when vaginal delivery is contraindicated, impossible, or associated with unacceptable risk.
99.conclusion:
Conclusion

Caesarean section is a major abdominal surgery that carries risks for both mother and baby and should be performed only when clinically justified. The nurse midwife must provide comprehensive pre-operative preparation, assist during the procedure, and deliver thorough post-operative care including vital sign monitoring, wound assessment, early ambulation, and breastfeeding support — all essential MUHS competencies that contribute to a safe surgical outcome and maternal recovery.

100.intro:
Introduction

Forceps delivery is an instrumental vaginal delivery technique in which obstetric forceps are applied to the fetal head to assist or accomplish delivery when the mother cannot push effectively or when the fetus is in distress. In Indian obstetric practice, forceps use requires a skilled operator and specific pre-conditions, and the nurse midwife must be familiar with the procedure to assist and provide appropriate care before and after delivery.
100.def:
Definition

Forceps delivery: An instrumental vaginal delivery in which obstetric forceps — a paired metal instrument with two blades that cradle the fetal head — are applied to the head and used to apply traction and, when necessary, rotation, to assist the birth of the baby during the second stage of labour.
100.conclusion:
Conclusion

Forceps delivery, when correctly indicated and technically executed, is a rapid and effective means of completing delivery in the second stage. The nurse midwife’s role is to recognise the prerequisites, prepare the woman and instruments, assist the operator, monitor the fetal heart rate throughout, and provide thorough post-delivery care to both mother and newborn — including neonatal assessment for birth trauma — as required by MUHS skilled-birth-attendant competencies.

101.intro:
Introduction

Vacuum (ventouse) extraction is an instrumental vaginal delivery technique that uses a suction cup applied to the fetal scalp to assist delivery during the second stage of labour. It is increasingly preferred over forceps in many Indian centres because it requires less anaesthesia, causes fewer maternal lacerations, and is easier to apply. The nurse midwife must understand its indications, prerequisites, and the immediate care required for the newborn following ventouse delivery.
101.def:
Definition

Vacuum (ventouse) extraction: An assisted vaginal delivery technique in which a suction cup is applied to the fetal scalp and negative pressure (vacuum) is created to form a chignon (artificial caput), allowing the operator to apply traction coordinated with maternal expulsive efforts to accomplish delivery of the fetus.
101.conclusion:
Conclusion

Vacuum extraction is a safe and effective method of assisted delivery when prerequisites are met and the cup is correctly applied. The nurse midwife must prepare the woman and equipment, assist during the procedure, monitor FHR throughout, and promptly assess the newborn for scalp injuries, cephalhaematoma, or intracranial bleeding after delivery. Thorough documentation and parental reassurance about the chignon are MUHS-expected nursing responsibilities.

102.intro:
Introduction

The Bishop score is the pre-induction scoring system used universally to assess cervical readiness before initiating labour artificially. A favourable (high) score predicts a successful induction with a short induction-to-delivery interval, while an unfavourable score signals the need for cervical ripening first. The nurse midwife must understand Bishop scoring to communicate cervical status accurately and assist in planning induction safely.
102.def:
Definition

Bishop score (pre-induction cervical score): A standardised scoring system that evaluates five cervical and pelvic parameters — cervical dilatation, effacement, consistency, and position, plus the station of the presenting part — each scored 0–3, giving a total of up to 13; a score above 6 indicates a favourable cervix suitable for induction of labour.
102.conclusion:
Conclusion

The Bishop score guides safe induction of labour by preventing unnecessary or failed inductions in women with an unripe cervix. The nurse midwife must be able to assess and report the five components accurately, recognise when cervical ripening agents (prostaglandin, Foley catheter) are needed before oxytocin, and monitor the woman closely throughout induction — core competencies tested in MUHS examinations and essential for safe maternity care under the NHM.
